data.title: OpenAI updates Agents SDK to improve agent safety and capability for enterprises
data.description:
OpenAI (OPENAI) released an updated version of its Agents Software Development Kit (SDK) that allows enterprises to build more capable agents able to work across multiple files and tools.

It also features a sandboxing ability that increases the safety of agents as they are contained in a controlled workspace. 

"Many useful agents need a workspace where they can read and write files, install dependencies, run code, and use tools safely," the company said. "Native sandbox support gives developers that execution layer out of the box, instead of forcing them to piece it together themselves."

This latest update comes about a week after OpenAI's chief rival, Anthropic (ANTHRO), launched Managed Agents.

"With today's release, the Agents SDK harness becomes more capable for agents that work with documents, files, and systems," OpenAI said. "It now has configurable memory, sandbox-aware orchestration, Codex-like filesystem tools, and standardized integrations with primitives that are becoming common in frontier agent systems." 

The update also gives developers an in-distribution harness for frontier models. This allows agents to work with files and approved tools within a workspace.

"The harness also helps developers unlock more of a frontier model's capability by aligning execution with the way those models perform best," OpenAI said. "That keeps agents closer to the model’s natural operating pattern, improving reliability and performance on complex tasks—particularly when work is long-running or coordinated across a diverse set of tools and systems."

These new Agents SDK capabilities are now available to all customers via the API and use standard API pricing, based on tokens and tool use. The new harness and sandbox abilities are now in Python but will receive TypeScript support in a future update.

data.title: The stock market is in a stronger position now than earlier this year – Fundstrat’s Tom Lee
data.description:
Fundstrat’s Tom Lee believes the stock market (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) is currently in a stronger position than when it reached all-time highs earlier this year, projecting the S&P 500 (SP500) can climb to 7,300 before experiencing any significant pullback.

The head of research and chief investment officer shared his optimistic outlook during an interview with CNBC, pointing to several factors that have bolstered his confidence in U.S. equities.

Lee cited three primary reasons for his bullish stance: the U.S. market’s resilience amid surging oil prices, rising corporate earnings, and a diminished threat from inflation.

“We now feel comfortable that the war is actually stimulating the economy,” Lee said, adding that historical analysis of oil spikes (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM) suggests the impact on core inflation may be less severe than previously anticipated.

The strategist characterized the U.S. as “the best house in a now more uncertain neighborhood,” noting that American factories continue operating while other nations face shutdowns.

Lee pointed out that this represents the seventh black swan event thrown at investors since 2020, yet the U.S. economy has demonstrated it “can handle a Middle East catastrophe” even when trade routes are restricted and oil availability is constrained.

While acknowledging that tail risks remain, Lee emphasized that “the size of the tail has shrunk.”

He noted that credible concerns exist around commodity shocks, including helium supply disruptions, but suggested a lasting ceasefire could create positive tail risks that would minimize any economic damage to U.S. shores.

Lee agreed with JPMorgan’s assessment that the technology sector must lead the next leg of the market rally. He highlighted that since the war began, the best-performing assets have been Ethereum (ETH-USD) and Bitcoin (BTC-USD)—which correlate highly with tech—followed by energy stocks (XLE), the Magnificent Seven (MAGS), and software companies (XSW), (IGV).

“Tech is growth when people are worried about growth,” Lee explained, adding that the sector has been the most under-owned group as investors sold off positions.

On valuations, Lee argued that falling prices have made tech stocks increasingly attractive relative to their earnings growth potential.

These companies possess “true moats” and have consistently outpaced S&P 500 (SP500) earnings growth, positioning them as primary beneficiaries of AI adoption.

“Five years from now, I think people are going to be surprised that they could buy them so cheaply,” he predicted.

data.title: Corebridge Financial appoints Christopher Filiaggi as interim CFO
data.description:
• Corebridge Financial (CRBG) announced on Wednesday the appointment of the company’s chief accounting officer, Christopher Filiaggi, as interim chief financial officer, effective April 24, 2026. Filiaggi will report to Marc Costantini, president and chief executive officer, and join the executive leadership team.
  • Filiaggi will serve as interim chief financial officer while the Company prepares for its planned merger with Equitable Holdings. In this role, he will provide continuity, disciplined execution, and steady financial leadership as Corebridge advances toward the combination.
  • Filiaggi will remain in the interim role until the closing of the Proposed Transaction, when Robin M. Raju, chief financial officer of Equitable Holdings, will serve as chief financial officer of the combined company.
  • Filiaggi has served as Chief Accounting Officer for Corebridge since June 2023, overseeing financial reporting, accounting policy, and internal controls. Before this role, he held finance leadership positions with Corebridge and American International Group. Previously, Filiaggi was with PricewaterhouseCoopers. 
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Investors should own commodities – Northwestern Mutual Wealth Management
data.description:
Brent Schutte, Chief Investment Officer at Northwestern Mutual Wealth Management, warns that investors have “bid back up the markets” by flocking to familiar names like the Magnificent Seven (MAGS) and nonprofitable tech stocks, a strategy he views as shortsighted.

Instead, the strategist recommends that investors “own some commodities” to hedge against what he sees as increasingly permanent inflation risks.

Schutte shared these insights during an interview with Bloomberg TV, where he discussed the potential for a “broadening economy” that could favor small- and mid-cap stocks over the mega-cap tech names that have dominated recent years.

He pointed to a shift that began in October when several major companies announced earnings and the Federal Reserve cut rates, prompting investors to consider opportunities beyond the usual market leaders.

The valuation disparity between large- and small-cap stocks is striking, according to Schutte. He noted that the S&P 600 (SP600) trades at roughly 14x to 15x earnings with an expected growth rate of around 17.5%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) commands 21x to 22x earnings for a similar growth rate.

“I like having valuation on my side of the trade because it can bail me out,” he said.

When it comes to portfolio construction, Schutte argues that the traditional 60/40 stock-and-bond mix needs to evolve.

“You need to own some commodities,” he advised, warning that inflation may be more of a “permanent thing” rather than transitory, as the economy hasn’t reached the Fed’s 2% target in five years.

The strategist outlined several risks on the horizon, including labor market growth concentrated heavily in health services and the potential for tariffs to rise again.

He also pointed to uncertainty surrounding Federal Reserve leadership and what he described as a “soap opera” in Washington over the potential appointment of Kevin Warsh as Fed chair, which could shift monetary policy in unpredictable directions.

Looking beyond near-term volatility, Schutte maintains his focus on assets that are “underinvested” and “underappreciated” by the broader market.

His long-term strategy centers on finding value in cheaper segments while acknowledging that short-term uncertainty remains elevated due to economic weakness and geopolitical conflict.

Gold and Gold Miner ETFs: (GLD), (IAU), (SGOL), (OUNZ), (BAR), (GDX), (GDXJ), (NUGT), (RING), and (DUST).

Silver and Silver Mining ETFs: (SLV), (SIVR), (AGQ), (ZSL), (PSLV), (SIL), (SILJ), and (SLVP).

Copper and Copper Mining ETFs: (CPER), (COPX), and (JJCTF).

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: Agereh Technologies announces $200K convertible debenture private placement
data.description:
• Agereh Technologies (AUTO:CA) plans a non-brokered private placement of convertible debentures to raise up to $200,000.
  • The debentures carry a 5% annual interest rate and mature in 24 months.
  • Each debenture is priced at $1,000 with interest payable at maturity.
  • Proceeds will be used for sales, marketing, and general working capital.
  • Debentures are convertible into units at $0.06 per unit before maturity.
  • Each unit includes one common share and one warrant exercisable at $0.07.
  • Warrants are valid for 24 months from issuance.

data.title: Euroseas extends charter for EM Kea at higher rate
data.description:
• Euroseas (ESEA) (NASDAQ: ESEA) on Wednesday said it has secured a three-year charter extension for its feeder containership EM Kea at a daily rate of $30,000.

  • The new contract will run for a minimum of 36 months, with an option to extend to 38 months, starting July 14, 2026, and represents an increase of around 60% from the vessel’s current rate.

  • The company said the extension is expected to generate about $22.5 million in EBITDA over the minimum contracted period and increases its charter coverage through 2028.
  • ESEA -0.11% after hours to $70.57.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: S&P 500 closes at record as investors price in positive Middle East developments
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages advanced on Wednesday, with the S&P 500 ((SP500)) ending at an all-time high as investors priced in good news on the U.S.-Iran conflict front while parsing more bank and financial earnings.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) closed at the record of 7,022.93 points, ending +0.8%.

The heavy-tech Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +1.6%, aided by Broadcom (AVGO), +4.3%, as it entered into a three-year partnership with Meta Platforms ((META)) to develop its AI chips and deploy them into data centers.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJI), however, finished -0.2%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 3 basis points higher at 4.28%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 1 basis point to 3.77%.

On geopolitics, President Donald Trump hinted that negotiations with Iran could take place “over the next two days” in Pakistan. This was after the U.S. announced a move towards a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

Reports surfaced that the U.S. and Iran may have made significant progress towards a framework agreement to end the war, according to U.S. officials. It was also reported that both countries are considering whether to extend their current ceasefire by two weeks to allow more time for peace negotiations.

“If these talks do result in some end to the conflict that the U.S. began, that would be a net positive from an economic standpoint. It would mean a reduction in oil prices, which we have started to see as this news is processed, which would reduce inflationary pressures that the U.S. was already dealing with because of tariffs,” said Seeking Alpha analyst Daniel Jones.

“However, I do think investors need to step back and look at the picture more broadly. Even before the conflict broke out, the U.S. was heading down the path toward a stagflationary recession. I don't see that changing even if market conditions improve. If anything, it will just take the edge off of the pain that we are experiencing.”

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were slightly lower at $91, while Brent (CO1:COM) was at $94 per barrel.

On the economy, U.S. export prices climbed faster than U.S. import prices in March, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

Also, the Empire State Manufacturing Index rose 11 points to 11.0 in April, vs. the -2.0 consensus, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York reported.

data.title: Southern First Bancshares launches public offering of common stock
data.description:
• Southern First Bancshares (NASDAQ: SFST) on Wednesday said it has launched an underwritten public offering of its common stock.
  • The company said it expects to grant underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 15% of the shares sold in the offering.
  • Proceeds will be used for general corporate purposes, including supporting growth initiatives, providing capital to its bank subsidiary, repaying debt and for working capital.
  • SFST -0.84% after hours to $58.3.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: What's next for Palantir as AI competition intensifies?
data.description:
Palantir Technologies (PLTR) has faced mounting pressure in 2026, with shares declining about 20% year-to-date following a 31% drawdown from recent peaks. The software company, which skyrocketed more than 150% in 2025 on surging demand for its AI platform, now faces headwinds as investors reassess competitive threats in the rapidly evolving AI landscape.

Despite the sharp pullback, analyst sentiment remains divided, with some viewing the selloff as an opportunity while others see deeper structural challenges ahead.

What Do Seeking Alpha Analysts Say About Palantir’s Future?

Arguments supportive to Palantir stress the company's robust fundamentals, highlighting Q4 revenue growth of 70% compared to last year and a 34% increase in customer count. Meanwhile, the firm's 2026 revenue guidance that came in 15% above consensus expectations. 

On the other hand, downside concerns center on competition from agile AI competitors like Anthropic, whose projected $30 billion annual recurring revenue surge threatens to erode Palantir’s middleware moat. Critics also pointed to lengthening deployment cycles and a fading “war premium” as concerns, along with persistent insider selling that has heightened downside risk.

Here’s a breakdown of what some analysts had to say:

  • Louis Gerard, Rating: Sell: “I believe that Palantir will go from the high-growth AI king to a mature enterprise software provider facing massive competitive headwinds. The combination of the astronomical valuation as well as the geopolitical rotation should provide a high-probability scenario for further downside.” - Palantir: Anthropic’s $30B ARR Surge Is Eating The Middleware Moat

  • Millennial Dividends, Rating: Hold: “The core of the investment thesis remains valuation (bears) and forward growth expectation (bulls). It's a tug of war situation. ... Don't put on the pink glasses just yet. Risks remain, competition from Anthropic won't fade away ...  I am willing to wait on the sidelines and see whether we see the shares moving towards $100/share, where I would be willing to pull the trigger." - Palantir: 3 Reasons That Could Pressure the Stock

  • Julia Ostian, Rating: Buy: “The company is a strong one operationally, and it has a crucial role in the U.S. defense, besides other governmental and commercial institutions. I believe the business is sticky and that it would be extremely difficult for peers to compete with Palantir. For the business reason alone, I rate it as a Buy." - Palantir: Anthropic Disruption Is An Illusion

What Do the Quant Ratings Say?

Palantir currently carries a rating from Seeking Alpha’s quant system that reflects the tension between strong growth fundamentals and valuation concerns. While the company continues to demonstrate impressive revenue expansion, multiple factors remain under pressure.

Valuation: The company continues to trade at a significant premium, well above sector medians. This elevated valuation remains a primary concern for value-oriented investors despite the recent drawdown.

Growth: Growth metrics stand out as a bright spot, with Q4 2025 revenue increasing 70% year-over-year and customer count expanding 34%. The company’s 2026 revenue guidance came in 15% above consensus demonstrates continued momentum.

Profitability: Palantir has demonstrated improving cash flow generation, though the 0.7% free cash flow yield remains modest relative to the company’s valuation multiples.

Momentum: Momentum has deteriorated significantly, with the 20% year-to-date drop reflecting the broader shift in investor sentiment from uncritical adoption of the AI story to concerns about competitive moats.

Earnings Revisions: The company’s 2026 guidance exceeding consensus by 15% has provided some support for forward estimates, though concerns about Anthropic competition and macro headwinds continue to weigh on analyst outlooks.

What’s the Latest News on Palantir?

The narrative surrounding Palantir has shifted dramatically as investors have moved from uncritical adoption of the AI story to scrutinizing the company’s competitive positioning. At the center of concerns is Anthropic’s emergence, which could threaten Palantir’s moat. PLTR's lengthening deployment cycles have added to concerns about its ability to maintain growth rates as competition intensifies.

Persistent macro headwinds from inflation and geopolitical instability have further complicated the picture, raising questions about the security of government contracts that have historically provided a stable revenue base for the company. The fading “war premium” that once supported Palantir’s valuation has become another point of concern as investors reassess the company’s long-term positioning.

data.title: Tether buys 951 BTC as Bitcoin rebounds to $75K monthly peak
data.description:
Bitcoin’s (BTC-USD) dip toward today's low near ~$73K didn’t stop one of its biggest corporate accumulators from adding more coins. On Wednesday, a Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reserve address linked with Tether (USDT-USD) moved ~951 BTC worth about ~$70.47M from Bitfinex (BFX-USD), as seen in the Arkham Intelligence chart.

The leading stablecoin issuer has been slowly building its Bitcoin (BTC-USD) reserve since 2023, using roughly ~15% of its profits to buy BTC and shifting holdings from Bitfinex (BFX-USD) after each quarter closes, reported Wu Blockchain.

With this latest transfer, Tether (USDT-USD) now holds around 97.141K BTC, worth nearly $7.23B, making it the fifth-largest Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holder on-chain.

What's even more interesting is that the buying activity comes at a time when Bitcoin (BTC-USD) has already bounced back to its recent monthly high around ~$75K. Overall, Tether (USDT-USD) continues to increase its exposure to crypto even during weak investor sentiment.
